reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This work introduces a fairness criterion named Equal Improvability (EI), which demands that for a binary classifier, the difficulty of flipping a prediction past the decision boundary be the same across all sensitive subgroups. To enforce EI, the authors propose three regularizers: one based on covariance, another using kernel density estimates, and a third derived from the loss landscape. Empirical results on benchmark datasets show that each of these penalties reduces EI disparity. Additionally, through experiments with synthetic data, the paper argues that EI contributes to sustained fairness over time under shifting distributions.

Strengths and Weaknesses:  
- Strength  
  • The paper tackles a timely and important challenge—ensuring fairness in machine learning classifiers.

- Weaknesses  
  • Novelty: The core idea behind EI closely mirrors the Equal Resource (ER) concept from Gupta et al. (2019). Concretely, EI’s formulation  
    P(max_{μ(Δx)≤δ} f(x+Δx)>0.5 | f(x)<0.5, z=z) = P(max_{μ(Δx)≤δ} f(x+Δx)>0.5 | f(x)<0.5)  
    or equivalently its expectation form, aligns with ER’s use of the same conditioning. The authors acknowledge prior work on fairness regularization in Section 3, yet there is no direct comparison to ER to illustrate where EI substantially departs or offers new insights. Moreover, the effect of outliers on EI has not been explored, even though ER’s sensitivity to such points is a noted concern.

  • Motivation–Method Mismatch: The abstract and introduction emphasize EI’s role in long-term fairness under dynamic shifts, but the optimization problem presented in Section 3 is static. Although Section 4.1 considers a temporal setting, the connection between its objective (4.1.1) and the foundational EI definition (Equation 1) is not clearly established.

  • Experimental Scope: Almost all experiments use a one-dimensional feature x, a binary sensitive attribute, and a small time horizon T. These toy settings do not reflect real-world scenarios where x is high-dimensional and there are multiple sensitive groups. Furthermore, it remains unclear why enforcing EI in these simple examples would translate into improved fairness in truly dynamic, high-dimensional applications.

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:  
The manuscript is generally well written. The notation for the proposed regularizers is precise, and the methods are described with clarity. The related literature is reviewed comprehensively. I did not identify any obvious barriers to reproducing the results based on the provided descriptions.

Overall Assessment:  
While the paper addresses an important question and is well presented, its conceptual contribution appears to be a somewhat incremental variant of existing work (ER), and the experimental validation is limited to overly simplistic setups. A more thorough comparison with Gupta et al. (2019) and tests on richer, higher-dimensional, multi-group scenarios would strengthen the submission. I would welcome a revision that addresses these points.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a new notion of group-level fairness, i.e., Equal Improvability (EI). The intuition behind the notion is that, by considering those who are rejected but are not too far away from the decision border, the effort taken by those to recourse should not vary among groups. The paper provides three empirical ways to (approximately) enforce EI in classification tasks. Empirical results in the simulated dynamics are also provided.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
## Strength

The proposed EI notion combines advantages of previous notions of a similar flavor (as summarized in Table 1), and makes an effort to avoid (some) problems of previous group-level fairness notions. Three different ways to (approximately) enforce EI are also provided. The authors demonstrate the benefit of considering EI in the dynamic setting in the experiments.

## Weakness

### 1. w.r.t. the distinction between zero-order vs. first-order fairness

I am wondering what is the "order" over here. Roughly, I think the paper is pointing out the fact that EI (among others, e.g., Equal Recourse, Bounded Effort) cares about the ability to change the status quo. However, since it is a shared characteristics among some previous notions, I am not sure what specific benefit is unique to EI. Also, this distinction is only made in the beginning of the paper, and was not mentioned after Section 2. It would be very helpful if authors can share some insight over here, especially for the purpose of long-term fairness, as claimed in the paper.

### 2. the unmentioned connection between individual-level recourse fairness and EI

If I understand it correctly, the central claim w.r.t. the advantage of EI compared to other notions is the potential to "equally" improve decision outcome for those who are currently rejected. This intuition is shared by, as pointed out in the paper, effort-based fairness notions. In my opinion, the intuition is actually more aligned with individual-level recourse fairness, e.g., von Kügelgen et al. (2022) "On the Fairness of Causal Algorithmic Recourse". EI claims to be more focused on those who are potentially able to recourse. However, the fact that EI remains a group-level fairness notion indicates that individual-level recourse fairness is more fine-grained and can better serve the intended purpose laid out by the authors. Additional discussion on the connection and difference would be very helpful.

### 3. w.r.t Definition 2.1 EI

I am wondering why we should limit our consideration to $\Delta x$ such that $\Delta_M x = 0$. According to the notion, by definition mutable features do not contribute to the cost of effort but can still alter the final prediction outcome. Then, I think it is more reasonable to encode such detail in the measurement of cost instead of forcing $\Delta_M x = 0$, since this will implicitly limit the possibility to recourse for certain individuals and in turn affect the estimation of recourse probability.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: Overall, the paper is not hard to follow. The results are relatively clearly presented. The paper can benefit from some additional illustrations/discussions of the claimed advantages of EI. The implementation details are provided in the paper for reproducibility.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The paper proposes a new group-level fairness notion and three ways to empirically enforce it in classification. There are some worries about the claimed advantages of EI, as well as the formulation of the definition of EI itself (as detailed in "Strength and Weakness"). It would be greatly appreciated if authors can kindly provide additional clarifications.

====== Post rebuttal =========

Thank authors for the response. I have updated my evaluation.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a new notion of group-level fairness, i.e., Equal Improvability (EI). The intuition behind the notion is that, by considering those who are rejected but are not too far away from the decision border, the effort taken by those to recourse should not vary among groups. The paper provides three empirical ways to (approximately) enforce EI in classification tasks. Empirical results in the simulated dynamics are also provided.

Strength And Weaknesses:
## Strength

The proposed EI notion combines advantages of previous notions of a similar flavor (as summarized in Table 1), and makes an effort to avoid (some) problems of previous group-level fairness notions. Three different ways to (approximately) enforce EI are also provided. The authors demonstrate the benefit of considering EI in the dynamic setting in the experiments.

## Weakness

### 1. w.r.t. the distinction between zero-order vs. first-order fairness

I am wondering what is the "order" over here. Roughly, I think the paper is pointing out the fact that EI (among others, e.g., Equal Recourse, Bounded Effort) cares about the ability to change the status quo. However, since it is a shared characteristics among some previous notions, I am not sure what specific benefit is unique to EI. Also, this distinction is only made in the beginning of the paper, and was not mentioned after Section 2. It would be very helpful if authors can share some insight over here, especially for the purpose of long-term fairness, as claimed in the paper.

### 2. the unmentioned connection between individual-level recourse fairness and EI

If I understand it correctly, the central claim w.r.t. the advantage of EI compared to other notions is the potential to "equally" improve decision outcome for those who are currently rejected. This intuition is shared by, as pointed out in the paper, effort-based fairness notions. In my opinion, the intuition is actually more aligned with individual-level recourse fairness, e.g., von Kügelgen et al. (2022) "On the Fairness of Causal Algorithmic Recourse". EI claims to be more focused on those who are potentially able to recourse. However, the fact that EI remains a group-level fairness notion indicates that individual-level recourse fairness is more fine-grained and can better serve the intended purpose laid out by the authors. Additional discussion on the connection and difference would be very helpful.

### 3. w.r.t Definition 2.1 EI

I am wondering why we should limit our consideration to $\Delta x$ such that $\Delta_M x = 0$. According to the notion, by definition mutable features do not contribute to the cost of effort but can still alter the final prediction outcome. Then, I think it is more reasonable to encode such detail in the measurement of cost instead of forcing $\Delta_M x = 0$, since this will implicitly limit the possibility to recourse for certain individuals and in turn affect the estimation of recourse probability.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Overall, the paper is not hard to follow. The results are relatively clearly presented. The paper can benefit from some additional illustrations/discussions of the claimed advantages of EI. The implementation details are provided in the paper for reproducibility.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper proposes a new group-level fairness notion and three ways to empirically enforce it in classification. There are some worries about the claimed advantages of EI, as well as the formulation of the definition of EI itself (as detailed in "Strength and Weakness"). It would be greatly appreciated if authors can kindly provide additional clarifications.

====== Post rebuttal =========

Thank authors for the response. I have updated my evaluation.

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This manuscript introduces a novel group-level fairness concept called Equal Improvability (EI). The core idea is that among individuals who are denied a positive decision but lie close to the classifier’s decision boundary, the amount of effort required to reverse that decision should be balanced across groups. The authors propose three practical algorithms to approximate EI in classification models and present simulation experiments demonstrating EI’s behavior in dynamic settings.

Strengths and Weaknesses  

Strengths  
- The EI definition integrates benefits from earlier related fairness measures (as outlined in Table 1) while attempting to sidestep certain drawbacks those measures exhibit.  
- The paper offers three distinct procedures to enforce EI approximately, giving practitioners multiple implementation options.  
- Experimental results illustrate how adopting EI can influence group outcomes over time, supporting the claim that it addresses dynamic fairness concerns.

Weaknesses  

1. Distinction Between “Zero-Order” and “First-Order” Fairness  
   - It remains unclear what “order” refers to in this context. While EI (and some prior notions like Equal Recourse or Bounded Effort) indeed focuses on individuals’ capacity to change their outcome, it isn’t obvious what unique benefit EI brings that earlier measures lack.  
   - Moreover, this zero- vs. first-order distinction is only briefly introduced in Section 1 and then never revisited. It would greatly clarify the manuscript if the authors elaborated on this terminology and its relevance—particularly regarding the paper’s claim about EI’s suitability for long-term fairness.

2. Relationship to Individual-Level Recourse Fairness  
   - The motivation for EI is to “evenly” enhance outcomes for those currently rejected, which mirrors the rationale behind effort-based individual recourse fairness (e.g., von Kügelgen et al. 2022).  
   - Because EI remains a group-based criterion, it is inherently coarser than individual-level recourse approaches, which can tailor recourse recommendations to each person. A discussion contrasting EI with individual recourse fairness—highlighting where EI adds value and where it may fall short—would strengthen the paper.

3. Definition 2.1 and the ΔM x = 0 Constraint  
   - The requirement that mutable features ΔM x remain zero seems unnecessarily restrictive. Under the presented framework, mutable features incur no cost but can still influence the classifier’s decision.  
   - Rather than forbidding changes to mutable attributes, it may be more principled to integrate them into the effort-cost function. Imposing ΔM x = 0 could unduly limit some individuals’ ability to gain recourse, thereby biasing the estimated recourse probabilities.

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility  
Overall, the manuscript is well organized and generally easy to follow. The empirical results are presented clearly. However, the paper would benefit from additional examples or schematic illustrations that underscore EI’s advantages over prior definitions. Implementation details are sufficiently described to allow for reproducibility.

Overall Recommendation  
This work proposes an interesting extension to group fairness by targeting balanced recourse effort across groups and offers practical enforcement techniques. Nonetheless, important conceptual clarifications—particularly regarding the “order” terminology, the link to individual recourse fairness, and the handling of mutable features—are needed. I encourage the authors to address these points thoroughly.

Post-Rebuttal Update  
I appreciate the authors’ detailed response. Based on the clarifications provided, I have revised my evaluation accordingly.

Final Rating  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Weighted Ensemble Self-Supervised Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies how ensemble methods can improve self-supervised learning algorithms. The considerations are limited to ensembles of projection heads and codebooks but not encoders, to ensure no extra computational cost involved. The authors propose a kind of data-dependent weighted cross-entropy losses. Two sota SSL methods, DINO and MSN are considered as baselines. In various experimental settings, the ensemble methods are observed to largely improve the baselines.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:
- This paper is well-written and easy to follow.
- The ensemble methods only incur a small training cost.
- It is interesting to see that sota SSL algorithms can be further improved by ensembling in various settings (linear eval, KNN, few-shot, etc.).

Weaknesses:
- The proposed framework only suits contrastive SSL methods (i.e., align positive pairs). Other SSL methods such as MAE can not be applied.
- When encoders are not considered for the ensemble, the possibilities for where to ensemble are not too many. The proposed ensemble framework somewhat lacks novelty. The main contribution is to test different weighting schemes.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
- Clarity: This paper is well-organized and easy to read.
- Novelty: The proposed ensemble framework seems to be a little bit simple. But the experiments are sufficient.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Overall,  the problem this paper studies is interesting. To simplify the design and retain the computational cost, this paper is limited to ensembles of projection heads and codebooks but not encoders, which limits the technical novelty. It is interesting to see the significant improvement in performance under various settings and evaluation metrics. Therefore, I think this is a borderline paper.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:
This manuscript investigates how ensembling can boost the performance of self-supervised learning (SSL) approaches without increasing encoder computation. Specifically, the authors restrict ensembles to projection heads and codebooks, introducing data-dependent weighted cross-entropy losses to combine their outputs. They evaluate their method on two leading SSL baselines—DINO and MSN—and demonstrate consistent gains across multiple evaluation protocols, including linear probing, KNN classification, and few-shot learning.

Strengths:
- The writing is clear and the presentation is straightforward.
- Incorporating the ensemble only over projection heads and codebooks adds minimal extra training cost.
- It is compelling to observe that current state-of-the-art SSL frameworks can be further improved via simple ensemble strategies across diverse downstream tasks.

Weaknesses:
- The proposed approach applies only to contrastive-style SSL objectives (i.e., methods that align positive pairs) and cannot extend to reconstruction-based methods like MAE.
- By excluding encoders from the ensemble, the design space is rather constrained; the core novelty largely resides in evaluating different weighting schemes rather than introducing fundamentally new techniques.

Clarity, Novelty, and Reproducibility:
- Clarity: The paper is well-structured and easy to follow.
- Novelty: While the ensemble concept is somewhat straightforward, the thorough experimental validation compensates for its simplicity.
- Reproducibility: The methods and settings are described in sufficient detail to allow replication.

Overall Assessment:
This work tackles an interesting question—can simple ensembling of projection modules enhance SSL without extra encoder cost? The restriction to heads and codebooks simplifies computation but also limits technical innovation. Nonetheless, the empirical results are strong and consistent under various metrics. On balance, I view this submission as borderline.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper systematically studies the ensemble of self-supervised learning. Ensemble has been one of the oldest time-proven strategies in machine learning and has been well-studied in supervised learning. Here, the authors introduce ensemble into the field of SSL. They mainly consider ensembling the projection head and codebook and leaving the encode part as future work. This proposed method is straightforward with rich analysis and proper results.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
## Strength
(1) The proposed method is straightforward and easy to implement with compelling results.

(2) I believe this paper includes some important and thorough experimental work on ensemble of self-supervised models.

(3) Detailed hyperparameters and training configurations are provided, improving the reproducibility.

## Weakness
(1) The ensembling of the projection head and codebook is a natural extension of the classifier ensemble in supervised models. It is not too surprising to see a performance improvement. 

(2) This paper mainly evaluated ensemble on transfer learning, while the performance on upstream tasks (Imagenet) has been overlooked.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:
The authors present a systematic investigation of ensembling strategies for self-supervised learning (SSL). While ensemble methods have a long history and are well established in supervised settings, their role in SSL remains underexplored. In this work, the authors focus on combining multiple projection heads and codebooks, deferring the ensemble of encoders to future research. The approach is straightforward, accompanied by detailed analyses and convincing experimental results.

Strengths:
1. The method is simple to implement and delivers strong empirical gains.
2. The paper offers an extensive and well-designed experimental study on ensembling in SSL.
3. Comprehensive hyperparameter settings and training details are provided, which greatly aid reproducibility.

Weaknesses:
1. Ensembling projection heads and codebooks closely parallels classifier ensembles in supervised learning, so the performance boost is somewhat expected rather than surprising.
2. The evaluation centers on transfer learning tasks, with no reported results on the upstream ImageNet benchmark.

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:
The submission makes a solid contribution, particularly in its empirical depth and the transparency of its experimental setup.

Overall Assessment:
Although the core idea—applying ensembling to projection heads and codebooks—does not constitute a major technical breakthrough, the paper offers an important and thorough empirical evaluation of SSL ensembling methods. I recommend acceptance, provided the authors address the noted shortcomings.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies the benefits of ensembling for self-supervised learning methods. The paper proposes to ensemble the “non-representation” part of the SSL models, and proposes different weighting schemes. Extensive experiments are conducted to analyze and evaluate the proposed ensemble method and weighting schemes, which lead to improvements over the non-ensemble models.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
- This could be a pretty timely work that explores ensembling for self-supervised learning models. I like the idea of only ensembling the non-representation part as that incurs little training overhead, plus no extra cost for downstream tasks.
- I appreciate that the authors included the analysis on the ensemble diversity. This gives a deeper understanding of the effectiveness of different weighting schemes, and could potentially guide the designs of future weighting schemes.
- The paper includes thorough analysis and ablations on the proposed methods, and shows strong empirical results compared to the baselines and SOTA.

Weaknesses:
- The connection between the Maximum Likelihood view of SSL and the proposed ensemble method design is not very clear to me. The presentation could be improved with stronger connections between Sec.2 and Sec.3.
- While the paper does a good job in evaluating a variety of ablations on the proposed ensemble method, it seems to lack other possible baselines for ensembling, e.g., instead of only ensembling the projection head, one baseline is to consider ensembling the base models. It’d be good to compare this baseline to understand whether there is a trade-off between the benefits of ensembling and the cost for extra parameters. In general, the paper should include comparisons to the other related ensemble baseline mentioned in Sec. 4.

Other questions/comments:
- Can one use the proposed ensembling method on pre-trained SSL models (e.g. DINO)? What’s the performance difference of that to applying ensembling in the pre-training from scratch? I think this is a relevant question to look into since there are already a lot of publicly available pre-trained SSL models, and it’d be best if the ensembling technique can be applied on top of these pre-trained models and still offer improvements.
- How does one set $\gamma$ for ENT in practice? From Figure 3.b, it seems to affect the performance pretty much.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This work investigates how ensembling can enhance self-supervised learning (SSL) approaches. Specifically, the authors propose to ensemble only the non-representation components (e.g., projection heads) of SSL architectures and introduce various schemes for weighting these ensembles. A broad set of experiments evaluates the proposed ensemble framework and weighting strategies, demonstrating consistent gains over single-model baselines.

Strengths  
- Relevance and Efficiency: The paper addresses a timely problem—applying ensembles to SSL—while cleverly limiting the ensemble to the non-representation modules. This design choice keeps additional training overhead minimal and does not add complexity to downstream tasks.  
- Diversity Analysis: Including an in-depth examination of ensemble diversity offers valuable insights into why some weighting schemes perform better than others. Such analysis can inform future designs of ensemble weighting methods.  
- Empirical Rigor: The authors carry out extensive ablation studies and comparisons against competitive baselines, yielding strong empirical improvements over state-of-the-art methods.

Weaknesses  
- Conceptual Link: The rationale connecting the Maximum Likelihood interpretation of SSL (Section 2) with the ensemble construction (Section 3) is somewhat tenuous. Strengthening the exposition to more explicitly tie these sections together would improve the paper’s coherence.  
- Baseline Comparisons: While the paper examines multiple ablations of its own method, it omits other natural ensemble baselines—most notably ensembling full base models rather than only the projection heads. A comparison here would help clarify any trade-offs between performance gains and the cost of extra parameters. More generally, the work should include comparisons to the related ensemble baselines mentioned in Section 4.

Additional Questions and Comments  
- Applicability to Pre-trained Models: Could the proposed ensemble strategy be applied to existing pre-trained SSL models (e.g., DINO) instead of training from scratch? Understanding the performance gap in that scenario would be valuable, especially given the prevalence of publicly available SSL checkpoints.  
- Hyperparameter Sensitivity: In practice, how should one choose the γ parameter for the ENT weighting scheme? Figure 3b suggests this choice significantly impacts results, so guidance on selecting γ would be helpful.

Clarity, Reproducibility, and Novelty  
- Clarity: The manuscript is generally well written and easy to follow.  
- Quality and Reproducibility: Experiments are comprehensive, with careful attention to baseline implementations. The authors provide sufficient experimental details and plan to release code, making reproduction feasible.  
- Novelty: To my knowledge, ensembling during SSL pre-training has not been thoroughly explored. The idea of ensembling only the non-representation portion, along with the proposed weighting schemes, is novel and empirically effective.

Overall Assessment  
This paper tackles an important and underexplored direction—ensembling for self-supervised learning—using a simple yet broadly applicable framework. Although it would benefit from comparisons to additional ensemble baselines to inform practical trade-offs, the existing experiments are thorough, and the gains are convincing. I therefore recommend acceptance.

Rating  
8: accept, good paper

reviews.text:
Review of “Downstream-Efficient Ensembles for Self-Supervised Models”

Summary  
This paper addresses ensembling strategies for self-supervised learning (SSL) models, an area less explored compared to supervised ensembles. The authors analyze where (which network components) and how to combine multiple SSL-trained models, offering a lightweight ensemble that merges only the prediction head and codebook via a weighted cross-entropy objective—leaving the shared encoder unchanged. They demonstrate notable gains in few-shot classification (e.g., 1% ImageNet-1k) over baseline SSL methods such as DINO and MSN.

Strengths  
1. Relevance: As self-supervised methods advance, investigating effective ensemble techniques is timely and important.  
2. Theoretical Foundations: The paper provides a clear maximum-likelihood interpretation of clustering-based SSL approaches, which solidly motivates the ensemble design.  
3. Practical Efficiency: By limiting ensembling to heads and codebooks rather than full encoders, the proposed scheme is computationally and storage-efficient.  
4. Clarity and Structure: The methodology is well-explained, and the experimental section offers sufficient detail to follow and reproduce the main claims.

Weaknesses and Questions  
1. Encoder Ensembling. While head/codebook fusion is effective, the community may be more interested in strategies that also ensemble encoder layers. How might your framework extend to combine full encoder representations?  
2. Generality Across SSL Paradigms. The experiments focus on dual-branch methods (DINO/MSN). Can the approach be applied to masked-image modeling (MIM) methods like MAE or other single-branch SSL architectures?  
3. Model Pool Construction. The paper is vague on how the ensemble members are obtained and diversified. What protocol ensures sufficient diversity among the heads/codebooks?  
4. Checkpoint Selection and Diversity Metrics. If the submodels come from intermediate training checkpoints, how do you choose which snapshots to ensemble? Have you considered ensembling heads from smaller backbones to boost diversity? Additionally, how does your method compare against “model soups” [1], which average multiple fine-tuned encoder weights without extra inference cost?  
5. Reporting Averages. In Table 3, it would help to include average performance numbers to make improvements more transparent.

Clarity, Novelty, and Reproducibility  
The paper is clearly written and contributes a novel, efficient ensemble approach for SSL heads. However, the experimental evaluation is somewhat narrow: it lacks exploration of full encoder ensembles and direct comparisons to existing weight-averaging ensembles.

Overall Assessment  
The idea of SSL-specific ensembling is promising, and the presented head-only fusion yields impressive few-shot gains. Yet, the work remains in an early stage: its applicability beyond dual-branch methods, strategies for encoder combination, methods for model diversification, and comparisons with prior ensembling techniques all require further development.  

Recommendation  
3: reject, not good enough

[1] Wortsman, et al. “Model soups: averaging weights of multiple fine-tuned models improves accuracy without increasing inference time.”

title: Unbiased Stochastic Proximal Solver for Graph Neural Networks with Equilibrium States

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper revisits the implicit and optimization perspective of designing graph neural networks. Based on this perspective, the paper adopts two existing algorithms such as stochastic proximal gradient descent and its variance-reduced version to accelerate the forward computation with sampling. The backward computation is simply replaced by a one-step backward on the equilibrium point. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed algorithm.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
# Strength:

The motivation and background of the studied problem have been clearly demonstrated, and the paper is well written. The paper revisits the implicit and optimization perspective of designing GNNs. The finite-sum reformulation draws a natural connection with stochastic proximal gradient algorithm and the variance-reduced variant. The idea is simple but effective and efficient as demonstrated in the experiments. 

# Weakness

The forward computation of the solver is natural, simple, and well-justified. However, the backward computation is simply replaced by a one-step backward on the equilibrium point. This seems surprising if it can work well in practice. It would be great if the author can provide solid theoretical justification as well as the empirical study on the gradient approximation error due to this approximation. Moreover, the idea of sampling is standard in stochastic optimization. Therefore, the contribution and novelty are a bit weak.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil: The paper is clearly written with good quality. The idea is simple but effective.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
The paper provides a simple but effective approach for the training of large-scale implicit GNNs. The effectiveness and efficiency are clearly demonstrated. Solid theoretical and empirical justification of the backward computation can further improve the paper.

## After rebuttal

The revision improves the paper. I am happy to increase my score.

reviews.text:
Paper Summary:  
This manuscript revisits graph neural network (GNN) design from an implicit/optimization standpoint. By reformulating the model as a finite-sum problem, the authors apply stochastic proximal gradient descent and its variance-reduced variant to speed up forward propagation via sampling. For backpropagation, they replace the full gradient computation with a single backward step taken at the equilibrium point. Experimental results show that this approach delivers both efficiency and strong predictive performance.

Strengths:  
- The paper presents a clear motivation and thoroughly reviews relevant background, making the problem context easy to follow.  
- Recasting implicit GNN inference as a finite-sum optimization naturally motivates the use of stochastic proximal gradient methods and their variance-reduced extensions.  
- The proposed strategy is conceptually simple, yet the experiments highlight its practical efficiency and effectiveness on large-scale tasks.  
- Overall, the writing is concise and well organized, facilitating reproducibility.

Weaknesses:  
- While the forward solver is justified convincingly, the one-step backward approximation at the equilibrium seems rather ad hoc. It is surprising that this crude approximation works so well in practice.  
- The authors should strengthen the theoretical underpinning for this backward step or, at minimum, include empirical analyses that quantify the gradient approximation error introduced by this shortcut.  
- Since sampling and variance reduction are standard tools in stochastic optimization, the paper’s novelty is somewhat limited. The contribution mainly lies in marrying these known techniques with implicit GNNs, rather than introducing fundamentally new algorithms.

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:  
The manuscript is clearly written and the presentation quality is high. The idea is straightforward but impactful, and the experiments appear reproducible given the detail provided.

Review Summary:  
This work offers a neat and efficient framework for training large-scale implicit GNNs. Its empirical performance is compelling, demonstrating clear gains in runtime and accuracy. However, a more solid theoretical or empirical account of the backward gradient approximation would further strengthen the contribution.

Post-Rebuttal Remarks:  
The authors’ revisions have addressed several of my concerns, improving the overall presentation and clarity. I am comfortable raising my assessment accordingly.

Rating:  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper revisits the implicit and optimization perspective of designing graph neural networks. Based on this perspective, the paper adopts two existing algorithms such as stochastic proximal gradient descent and its variance-reduced version to accelerate the forward computation with sampling. The backward computation is simply replaced by a one-step backward on the equilibrium point. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed algorithm.

Strength And Weaknesses:
# Strength:

The motivation and background of the studied problem have been clearly demonstrated, and the paper is well written. The paper revisits the implicit and optimization perspective of designing GNNs. The finite-sum reformulation draws a natural connection with stochastic proximal gradient algorithm and the variance-reduced variant. The idea is simple but effective and efficient as demonstrated in the experiments. 

# Weakness

The forward computation of the solver is natural, simple, and well-justified. However, the backward computation is simply replaced by a one-step backward on the equilibrium point. This seems surprising if it can work well in practice. It would be great if the author can provide solid theoretical justification as well as the empirical study on the gradient approximation error due to this approximation. Moreover, the idea of sampling is standard in stochastic optimization. Therefore, the contribution and novelty are a bit weak.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper is clearly written with good quality. The idea is simple but effective.

Summary Of The Review:
The paper provides a simple but effective approach for the training of large-scale implicit GNNs. The effectiveness and efficiency are clearly demonstrated. Solid theoretical and empirical justification of the backward computation can further improve the paper.

## After rebuttal

The revision improves the paper. I am happy to increase my score.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: Two unbiased stochastic proximal solvers for learning graph equilibrium models are proposed. They are inspired by the stochastic proximal gradient descent method and its variance reduction variant (called USP and USP-VR solver). Both provide considerable computational speed-ups in comparison with the original solvers.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
+ A simple, computationally less expensive to optimize graph equilibrium models is proposed.
+ The authors provide convergence guarantees of the average proposed sampling method and derive its convergence rate (O(1/t))
+ They also propose a simple variance reduction scheme that enjoys a linear convergent rate like the vanilla deterministic solvers.

Weaknesses and open questions:
- The claimed performance improvements in Table 2 are often not significant.
- How does the run time (in Figure 2) compare to explicit methods (which often do not perform much worse).
- The equilibrium solution $Z^*$ needs to be defined precisely. Currently, the formulation of Proposition 1 suggests that $Z^*$ might be unique, which is not the case in general. Also the experiments confirm this, since the IGNN variants do not seem to converge to the same solutions.
- How is the proposal different from (Chen et al., 2018; Hamilton et al., 2017) for "traditional graph models" and why should these not transfer to graph equilibrium models?
- The convergence results in expectation seem trivial as, in expectation, the procedure is not different from optimising the full model. The real challenge would lie in the analysis of the error that is introduced by the sampling scheme (or the average number of additional SGD steps to reach an equilibrium that are required for the actual sampling scheme and not its average).
- A complexity analysis of the proposed algorithm would strengthen the claim of computational advantages.

Points of minor critique and open questions:
- Examples for f on page 3 would be appreciated.
- Table 3 does not report any significance intervals.
- The formulation is restricted to symmetric weight structure $WW^T$ but the authors claim that this has no effect on the performance of the model. (It should impact its expressive power though...)
- In contrast to what the authors claim the weight normalisation will influence the training dynamics.
- page 5/6: `Therefore, our USP-VR solver can achieve a more accurate solution compared with the USP-VR solver with limited propagation times in the forward procedure.' does not seem to make sense.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
The paper does not make a very polished impression and is for that reason hard to follow from time to time.
It should be discussed more concretely why previous cannot be transferred to the setting of equilibrium because this does not seem to be apparent.
The proposed scheme seems relatively trivial but this can also be a feature.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
A strength of the paper is that the methodological proposals achieve performance speed-ups that are backed up by theoretical investigations.
It is not sufficiently clear to me, however, whether the contribution is sufficiently novel in comparison with similar schemes that have been developed for "non-equilibrium" models.
Furthermore, the presentation of the paper could be improved.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
Two unbiased stochastic proximal solvers for learning graph equilibrium models are proposed. They are inspired by the stochastic proximal gradient descent method and its variance reduction variant (called USP and USP-VR solver). Both provide considerable computational speed-ups in comparison with the original solvers.

Strength And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
+ A simple, computationally less expensive to optimize graph equilibrium models is proposed.
+ The authors provide convergence guarantees of the average proposed sampling method and derive its convergence rate (O(1/t))
+ They also propose a simple variance reduction scheme that enjoys a linear convergent rate like the vanilla deterministic solvers.

Weaknesses and open questions:
- The claimed performance improvements in Table 2 are often not significant.
- How does the run time (in Figure 2) compare to explicit methods (which often do not perform much worse).
- The equilibrium solution $Z^*$ needs to be defined precisely. Currently, the formulation of Proposition 1 suggests that $Z^*$ might be unique, which is not the case in general. Also the experiments confirm this, since the IGNN variants do not seem to converge to the same solutions.
- How is the proposal different from (Chen et al., 2018; Hamilton et al., 2017) for "traditional graph models" and why should these not transfer to graph equilibrium models?
- The convergence results in expectation seem trivial as, in expectation, the procedure is not different from optimising the full model. The real challenge would lie in the analysis of the error that is introduced by the sampling scheme (or the average number of additional SGD steps to reach an equilibrium that are required for the actual sampling scheme and not its average).
- A complexity analysis of the proposed algorithm would strengthen the claim of computational advantages.

Points of minor critique and open questions:
- Examples for f on page 3 would be appreciated.
- Table 3 does not report any significance intervals.
- The formulation is restricted to symmetric weight structure $WW^T$ but the authors claim that this has no effect on the performance of the model. (It should impact its expressive power though...)
- In contrast to what the authors claim the weight normalisation will influence the training dynamics.
- page 5/6: `Therefore, our USP-VR solver can achieve a more accurate solution compared with the USP-VR solver with limited propagation times in the forward procedure.' does not seem to make sense.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The paper does not make a very polished impression and is for that reason hard to follow from time to time.
It should be discussed more concretely why previous cannot be transferred to the setting of equilibrium because this does not seem to be apparent.
The proposed scheme seems relatively trivial but this can also be a feature.

Summary Of The Review:
A strength of the paper is that the methodological proposals achieve performance speed-ups that are backed up by theoretical investigations.
It is not sufficiently clear to me, however, whether the contribution is sufficiently novel in comparison with similar schemes that have been developed for "non-equilibrium" models.
Furthermore, the presentation of the paper could be improved.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This manuscript introduces two unbiased stochastic proximal algorithms—USP and its variance-reduced version USP-VR—for training graph equilibrium models. Both methods draw inspiration from stochastic proximal gradient descent and its variance-reduction counterpart, and they claim to accelerate computation compared to the original deterministic solvers.

Strengths  
+ The paper offers a straightforward, lower-cost optimization approach for graph equilibrium models.  
+ It establishes convergence guarantees for the average of the proposed random sampling scheme, proving an O(1/t) rate.  
+ The authors further present a simple variance-reduction strategy (USP-VR) that attains linear convergence, on par with the standard deterministic solvers.

Weaknesses and Open Questions  
- In Table 2, the reported speed-ups are often modest and not always statistically compelling.  
- Figure 2 presents run times for the new methods, but it remains unclear how they compare against explicit alternatives, which frequently perform similarly.  
- The equilibrium solution Z* is not rigorously defined. Proposition 1 seems to imply uniqueness of Z*, yet in general multiple equilibria can exist. Indeed, the experiments show that different IGNN variants converge to different fixed points.  
- It is not clear how this work differs from the solvers of (Chen et al., 2018) or (Hamilton et al., 2017) developed for “traditional” graph models, nor why those approaches would fail to extend to equilibrium models.  
- The convergence results stated “in expectation” appear trivial, since on average the algorithm reduces to full-model optimization. A deeper analysis of the sampling error—or of the extra number of inner SGD steps required by the actual sampler versus its expectation—would be more illuminating.  
- A formal complexity analysis is missing; such an analysis would substantiate the claimed computational benefits.

Minor Points and Clarifications  
- It would help to include concrete examples of the function f on page 3.  
- Table 3 lacks any confidence intervals or measures of significance.  
- The framework assumes a symmetric weight form (W Wᵀ), yet the authors assert this has no impact on performance—though it should affect expressive capacity.  
- Contrary to the authors’ claim, weight normalization does influence training dynamics.  
- The sentence on pages 5–6 (“Therefore, our USP-VR solver can achieve a more accurate solution compared with the USP-VR solver with limited propagation times in the forward procedure.”) is confusing and needs rewording.

Clarity, Novelty, and Reproducibility  
The presentation feels somewhat rough and occasionally hard to follow. A more concrete discussion of why existing solvers for non-equilibrium graph models fail in the equilibrium setting would strengthen the manuscript. Although the proposed scheme is relatively simple, that simplicity could also be viewed as an advantage.

Overall Assessment  
On the plus side, the authors deliver performance improvements that are corroborated by theoretical arguments. However, the paper’s novelty over analogous variance-reduction or stochastic proximal techniques for non-equilibrium models is not entirely convincing, and the writing could be refined.

Rating  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper proposed an efficient way to compute Graph Equilibrium models. The computation of the Graph Equilibrium model is an optimization problem of a graph denoising problem, and its naive solution is computationally expensive due to the use of an entire graph. This paper introduced the Unbiased Stochastic Proximal Solver (USP-solver) and its variance-reduction version USP-VR. They compute the Graph Equilibrium model by sampling the edges of the underlying graph. It was shown that USP converges to the equilibrium state at a sub-linear rate and USP-VR at a linear rate. Numerical experiments were conducted on node prediction and graph prediction problems to verify the practical usefulness of the proposed methods.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
- The proposed methods have guarantees for convergence.
- Numerical experiments demonstrated the usefulness of the proposed methods on large graph datasets.

Weaknesses
- Novelty in methodology is somewhat limited because randomization and variance reduction are relatively standard approaches to improve optimization algorithms.
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity

It took me some time to understand the method on first reading because (1) looks like one layer of an ordinal GNN, although the main interest of this paper is implicit GNNs. Therefore, I would suggest briefly writing the formulation of implicit GNNs, and how (1) is derived.
Nevertheless, overall, the paper is well-written.


Quality

The proposed methods are justified from both theoretical and empirical points of view. First, Propositions 1 and 2 ensure that the proposed methods converge to an equilibrium state, demonstrating their theoretical soundness. Regarding the empirical evaluations, I want to clarify what the authors meant by computational complexity (e.g., in the abstract). Figure 2, Table 3, and Figure 3 certainly provide evidence for the improvement of numerical improvements in computational speed. They have also shown that there is no degradation in prediction performance. If the authors also intended space complexity, it would be desirable to evaluate memory usage quantitatively. That said, memory efficiency is implicitly evaluated, although I am not sure the authors intended this. For example, in Table 2, when using the Reddit dataset, the vanilla IGNNs caused OOM, while IGNN+USP and IGNN+USP-VR did not. 

Novelty

If my understanding is correct, randomization is a relatively standard approach to reduce the computational complexity of optimization algorithms in expectation or with high probability. Also, variance reduction is a standard technique. In this sense, the method proposed in this paper is a natural improvement of the algorithm.

Reproducibility

The Appendix described the hyperparameters of the prediction model. Although this paper did not describe every full detail of dataset preparations, this is not a problem because references and dataset specifications are available. Since code is not provided, there is no guarantee of perfect reproduction. However, I think we can implement the code to reproduce the experiments to some degree.

Minor Comments

- P3 (1): $Z^{(n)}$, $Z^{(n+1)}$, $Z$ are undefined.
- P4 $\|WW^\top\| \leq 1/\tilde{A}$: this formula is invalid because the left hand side is scaler-valued, while the right hand side is vector-valued.
- P4 (3): $g(Z) + f(Z)$ -> $\min_{Z} g(Z) + f(Z)$
- P6 last equation: $\sum_{(k1, j) \in \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_k}$ -> $\sum_{(k_11, j) \in \tilde{\mathcal{E}}_k}$
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Although randomization and variance reduction are relatively standard approaches, as far as I know, these approaches have no application to implicit GNNs. In addition, the method is sound in that it has theoretical guarantees, and numerical experiments have shown its practical usefulness.

The responses by the authors solved my questions. Therefore, I want to keep my score, leaning to accept.

reviews.text:
Paper Summary:
The authors present an efficient approach for solving Graph Equilibrium models, which arise as a denoising optimization problem on graphs. Directly optimizing over the entire graph is computationally heavy, so they introduce the Unbiased Stochastic Proximal Solver (USP-solver) and its variance-reduction variant (USP-VR), both of which operate by randomly sampling graph edges. The paper proves that USP converges to the equilibrium at a sublinear rate and that USP-VR achieves linear convergence. Experiments on node-level and graph-level prediction tasks demonstrate that these methods significantly accelerate computation without sacrificing predictive accuracy.

Strengths and Weaknesses:
Strengths
- Both algorithms come with rigorous convergence guarantees.
- Empirical results on large-scale graphs confirm the practical effectiveness of the proposed solvers.

Weakness
- The methodological innovations are somewhat incremental, since randomness and variance-reduction are well-established techniques in optimization.

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:

Clarity
On first reading, equation (1) initially resembles a single layer of a conventional GNN, even though the core contribution lies in implicit GNN formulations. I recommend adding a concise derivation of the implicit GNN framework and showing how (1) follows from it. Otherwise, the paper is clearly written.

Quality
The theoretical foundations are solid: Propositions 1 and 2 prove convergence to equilibrium. Empirically, Figures 2 and 3 and Table 3 convincingly show faster runtimes, and there is no drop in prediction metrics. The abstract’s reference to “computational complexity” could be clarified—does it refer solely to time complexity, or also to memory usage? If space complexity is intended, reporting memory consumption would strengthen the claim. Although not explicitly measured, Table 2 hints at improved memory efficiency: on the Reddit dataset, standard IGNNs run out of memory (OOM), whereas IGNN+USP and IGNN+USP-VR complete successfully.

Novelty
Applying random sampling and variance reduction to implicit GNN optimization is a natural extension of standard optimization heuristics. While these techniques are familiar, their adoption in this setting appears novel.

Reproducibility
The appendix lists the prediction-model hyperparameters. Dataset preparation details are not fully spelled out, but references are provided, making it feasible to reconstruct. Because the authors have not released code, exact reproduction isn’t guaranteed, yet the methodology is described well enough to implement.

Minor Comments
- Page 3, equation (1): the variables Z⁽ⁿ⁾, Z⁽ⁿ⁺¹⁾, and Z are not defined.
- Page 4: the inequality ‖W Wᵀ‖ ≤ 1/Ã is invalid: the left side is scalar, the right side appears vector-valued.
- Page 4, equation (3): should read “min₍Z₎ g(Z)+f(Z)” rather than “g(Z)+f(Z)”.
- Page 6, final equation: the index (k1, j) in ∑₍(k1, j)∈Ėₖ₎ should be (k₁₁, j).

Overall Assessment:
Although randomization and variance reduction themselves are established, their use in implicit GNNs is, to my knowledge, novel. The algorithms are backed by solid theory and validated empirically on large graphs.

Post-Rebuttal Remarks:
The authors’ responses addressed all of my questions, so I maintain my original score and remain inclined to accept.

Rating:
6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

reviews.text:
Review Summary:
This manuscript proposes an optimization‐driven sampling scheme for graph neural networks, aiming to cut the computational expense of repeated graph diffusion steps. The authors also incorporate a variance reduction strategy to further boost performance.

Strengths:
• The paper presents a clear technique to speed up Implicit GNNs (IGNNs).

Weaknesses:
• The contribution is narrowly focused on IGNN and feels incremental. There exist many other GNN families—such as those based on ODEs or PDEs—and if every specific architecture requires its own sampling tweak, we risk a deluge of minor extensions. It’s not obvious that this work offers a broadly inspiring direction or fills a critical gap for the wider GNN community.

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:
The writing is lucid and the experimental setup is sound, making the work easy to follow and reproduce. Nevertheless, the core idea lacks significant novelty and feels like a modest adaptation rather than a substantial advance.

Overall Assessment:
In light of the limited scope and incremental nature of the contribution, I am inclined to recommend rejection. I remain open to other perspectives before reaching a final verdict.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper  
This work studies adversarial linear MDPs and introduces what appears to be the first computationally efficient algorithm that attains a √K regret guarantee. In addition, the authors provide a matching lower bound. Their approach builds on the occupancy-measure framework introduced by Jin et al. (2020b). The key technical innovation is the introduction of a carefully designed “imaginary next state,” which breaks the dependence between actual successor states and enables a standard linear regression procedure to estimate the parameter θ.

Strengths and Weaknesses  
Strengths:  
• The theoretical guarantees are strong, delivering a √K upper bound and a corresponding lower bound.  
• The notion of an “imaginary next state” is an interesting and, to my knowledge, novel idea.  
• The result tightens dependence on horizon length H and state-space size S relative to Jin et al. (2020b).  

Weaknesses:  
• Several of the new technical ingredients are not fully spelled out in the main text, making it hard to appreciate their novelty and role.

Comments on Clarity, Novelty, and Reproducibility  
The paper is generally well written, with clear motivations and clean proofs. However, I believe a few of the more subtle technical contributions deserve deeper discussion:

1. Choice of the Imaginary Next State (Eqn. 2)  
   The paper mentions selecting the next-state surrogate “for some technical reasons,” but this step seems far from trivial. It resembles an implicit exploration strategy—perhaps choosing the successor that maximizes uncertainty. A brief explanation or motivation for this choice would help readers understand why it works.

2. Remark 2 and Additional Techniques  
   In Remark 2 the authors note that extending the transition estimator from the imaginary next-state idea is “not straightforward,” implying there are further nontrivial steps to remove dependencies. It would be beneficial to outline these additional tweaks in the main text rather than relegating them to supplementary remarks.

3. Section 4.2 Length  
   Section 4.2 largely recounts standard tools from prior work. A more concise presentation—perhaps referring the reader to a brief appendix for well-known arguments—would streamline the flow and focus attention on the novel parts.

Overall Assessment  
This paper delivers meaningful advances in adversarial linear MDPs, both in terms of regret bounds and algorithmic ideas. While its core contributions are promising, some of the new techniques are not sufficiently explained in the body of the paper. Clarifying these points would strengthen the reader’s appreciation of the work.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper studies adversarial linear mixture MDPs with unknown transition and bandit feedback. The authors propose an algorithm with an upper bound $\tilde{O}(dS^2 \sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$, and prove an almost near-matching lower bound (in $d, K, A$). The authors highlight the use of a novel occupancy measure difference lemma for linear mixture MDPs, which might be of independent interest.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

(+) The upper bound is accompanied by a near-matching lower bound. The authors also justify the dependence on $S$ and $A$ via the lower bound.

(+) The use of a novel occupancy measure difference lemma (Appendix B.1).

Weaknesses:

(-) The bound depends on $S$, which is unusual and undesirable given the linear mixture MDPs assumption.

(-) The writing can be improved, especially the main results.

Detailed comments:

- I am familiar with statistical RL theory, but not so much in the adversarial setting. Hence, I will comment more on the writing/correctness aspects of the paper rather than the novelty/contribution.

- (main concern) Since the upper bound in Theorem 1 has a dependence on both $S$ and $A$, would this be just the tabular setting? I don’t quite understand where the linear approximation comes in when we still have the dependence on $S$.

- I encourage the authors to make it more explicit which elements are novel, particularly in your algorithm design. For example, how does your proposed algorithm differ from Jin et al. (2020b)?

- The main results (Theorem 1 and 2) should be made more detailed and self-contained. I encourage the authors to at least briefly introduce the settings and various relevant notations in the Theorems.

- The lower bound contains a term $dH\sqrt{K}$, which is linear in the horizon $H$. However, the upper bound only has a $\sqrt{H}$ dependence. Would this lead to a contradiction, or am I missing something here?
reviews.clarity_quality_novelty_and_reproducibil:
Clarity: The paper is generally well-written and easy to follow. The literature review and comparison with existing works are sufficient.

Novelty: The analysis in the paper is sufficiently novel. The analysis utilizes a novel occupancy measure difference (Lemma 2), which is a non-trivial extension from Jin et al. (2020b). Although the tightness of Lemma 2 is unknown, it may be of independent interest and useful for the community.  

Summary Of The Review: The paper studies adversarially linear mixture MDPs. The main results are interesting with a relatively novel analysis. However, the implications of the main results are unclear: what did we gain from the linear MDPs assumptions if the upper bound still depends on $S$?

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This work tackles adversarial linear mixture Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) with unknown transition kernels and only bandit feedback. The authors present an algorithm achieving an upper regret bound of order Õ(d S² √K + √(H S A K)), and they complement this with a nearly matching lower bound in terms of d, K, and A. A key technical ingredient is a new occupancy-measure difference lemma for linear mixture MDPs, which could be of standalone interest.

Strengths:  
(+) The paper provides both an upper bound and a near-tight lower bound, and uses the lower bound to argue that the dependencies on S and A are necessary.  
(+) The introduction and proof of the occupancy-measure difference lemma (Appendix B.1) represent a nontrivial theoretical contribution.

Weaknesses:  
(–) The regret bound still scales with the number of states S, which is surprising under the linear mixture assumption and somewhat undermines the benefit of linear generalization.  
(–) The exposition, particularly around the main theorems, could be clearer and more self-contained.

Detailed Comments:  
- I have a strong background in statistical reinforcement learning but less experience with adversarial settings, so my feedback will emphasize clarity and correctness over application novelty.  
- My primary concern is the S-dependence in Theorem 1’s upper bound. If the regret still grows with S, is this truly leveraging the linear structure, or does it simply reduce to a tabular result? I would like a clearer explanation of how linear function approximation plays a role here.  
- Please clarify exactly which components of your algorithm are new compared to previous work (e.g., Jin et al. 2020b). What are the novel design choices or analysis steps?  
- The statements of Theorems 1 and 2 would benefit from being more self-contained: briefly recall the setting, define all notation used, and state assumptions clearly within the theorem.  
- In the lower bound, there is a d H √K term that scales linearly with H, whereas your upper bound scales as √H. This seems inconsistent—am I misreading, or is there a gap to address?

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:  
Clarity: Overall the paper is reasonably well written, and the literature discussion is adequate, but sections describing the main results need more detail.  
Novelty: The analytical approach, especially the new occupancy-measure difference lemma, is a meaningful extension beyond Jin et al. (2020b) and could be useful to the community. The tightness of this lemma remains an open question, however.

Overall Assessment:  
This manuscript makes an interesting contribution to adversarial linear mixture MDPs by offering a novel analytic tool and near-optimal bounds. Nonetheless, it is unclear what is gained from the linear mixture setting if the regret still depends on the number of states S, and the algorithmic innovations over existing methods are not spelled out in detail.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies inhomogeneous adversarial linear mixture MDPs. It presents an efficient algorithm called LSUOB-REPS which provably achieves $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d S^2 \sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$. The algorithm relies on occupancy measure based techniques, which maintains a confidence set of the unknown transition function and runs online mirror descent over the space of occupancy measures. One novelty is the construction of a specific linear regression objective function to solve for the transition parameter. To this end, this paper proposes to use an imaginary next state to construct the regression target. Furthermore, a lower bound of order $\Omega(dH\sqrt{K} + \sqrt{HSAK})$ is proven, which shows that the algorithm has close-to-optimal performance.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
Strength:

1. This paper gives the first answer to a quite challenging problem, i.e., the adversarial linear MDPs. Both an upper bound and a lower bound (under certain assumptions) are given.

2. The paper is very well-written. This is a very technical paper. Techniques are presented with rather detailed explanations, making it easier for the readers to follow.

3. The paper has some novelty. The technical challenges are also discussed quite thoroughly, showing the technical contributions of the paper clear. For example, the imaginary next-state seems like a quite interesting idea to deal with the challenge of constructing a confidence ellipsoid when the classical self-normalized concentration technique cannot be directly applied. 

---
Weakness: 

There is no major weakness as far as I can tell. There are some comments though. 
1. The dependence of the upper bound on $|S|$ is unpleasant under the linear mixture MDP setting. 

2. The paper lacks a very detailed comparison with [1]. Although it seems that [1] studies tabular MDPs which is different from linear mixture MDP, the major idea of algorithmic design is very similar. Furthermore, in the case of representing a tabular MDP by a linear mixture MDP, we would have a relation $d = |S|^2 |A|$. In this case the bound in this paper would actually be worse than that of $1$ (but I can understand this since representing a tabular MDP by a linear mixture is inefficient. Still a more detailed comparison would be good to have).

3. There is no discussion on computational efficiency of the algorithm, so it might be inappropriate to call the algorithm ‘efficient’.    

[1] Jin, Chi, et al. "Learning adversarial markov decision processes with bandit feedback and unknown transition." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2020.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Paper:  
This work tackles inhomogeneous adversarial linear mixture MDPs by introducing an algorithm named LSUOB-REPS. The authors prove that their method achieves a regret bound of Õ(d S² √K + √(H S A K)). The approach builds on occupancy-measure techniques, maintaining a confidence set for the unknown transition kernel and applying online mirror descent over occupancy measures. A key novelty lies in designing a tailored linear regression objective for estimating the transition parameters, which uses a constructed “imaginary” next state to form the regression target. Additionally, the paper establishes a lower bound of Ω(d H √K + √(H S A K)), demonstrating the near-optimality of the proposed algorithm.

Strengths:  
1. This is the first work to address adversarial linear MDPs in a comprehensive way, providing both matching upper and lower bounds (under the paper’s assumptions).  
2. The manuscript is very well written. Despite its technical depth, the authors offer detailed explanations of the main techniques, which aids comprehension.  
3. There are genuine technical innovations—for example, the imaginative use of an artificial next state to construct a confidence ellipsoid when standard self-normalized concentration proofs do not directly apply.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:  
1. The regret’s dependence on the state-space size |S| in the upper bound is somewhat undesirable in a linear mixture setting.  
2. A more in-depth comparison with Jin et al. (ICML 2020) [1] would strengthen the paper. Although that work focuses on tabular MDPs, its algorithmic ideas bear resemblance. In particular, if one forced a tabular MDP into the linear mixture framework, one obtains d = |S|² |A|, in which case the bound here can be worse than that of [1]. I understand this inefficiency is inherent, but a clearer discussion would help.  
3. The paper does not discuss the algorithm’s computational cost. Without such an analysis, calling the method “efficient” may be misleading.

Clarity, Quality, Novelty, and Reproducibility:  
Clarity: Overall the paper is lucid. Theorems are stated unambiguously, technical hurdles and their resolutions are explained in a coherent manner, and related work is appropriately reviewed.  
Quality: As a theoretical contribution, the theorems appear sound, and I found no obvious flaws (though I have not vetted every detail).  
Novelty: The paper offers innovative ideas—especially how it constructs a confidence set in the adversarial scenario and chooses the imaginary next state—which I find valuable.  
Reproducibility: Given this is purely theoretical, there are no experiments. The proofs are presented clearly enough that an interested reader should be able to follow and verify them.

Overall Recommendation: Accept  
I endorse acceptance for the following reasons:  
1. Strong theoretical contribution: This paper resolves a challenging question in adversarial reinforcement learning.  
2. Original technical ideas: The methods introduced to overcome key obstacles are both clever and clearly articulated.

Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)

For example, the imaginary next-state seems like a quite interesting idea to deal with the challenge of constructing a confidence ellipsoid when the classical self-normalized concentration technique cannot be directly applied. 

---
Weakness: 

There is no major weakness as far as I can tell. There are some comments though. 
1. The dependence of the upper bound on $|S|$ is unpleasant under the linear mixture MDP setting. 

2. The paper lacks a very detailed comparison with [1]. Although it seems that [1] studies tabular MDPs which is different from linear mixture MDP, the major idea of algorithmic design is very similar. Furthermore, in the case of representing a tabular MDP by a linear mixture MDP, we would have a relation $d = |S|^2 |A|$. In this case the bound in this paper would actually be worse than that of $1$ (but I can understand this since representing a tabular MDP by a linear mixture is inefficient. Still a more detailed comparison would be good to have).

3. There is no discussion on computational efficiency of the algorithm, so it might be inappropriate to call the algorithm ‘efficient’.    

[1] Jin, Chi, et al. "Learning adversarial markov decision processes with bandit feedback and unknown transition." International Conference on Machine Learning. PMLR, 2020.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
Clarity: This paper is clear in general.Theorems have no ambiguity to me. Technical challenges and the corresponding solutions are described clearly. Related work is also discussed properly with now ambiguity. 


Quality: The quality is good. This is a purely theoretical paper and the theorems are solid. The proof feels correct to me, though I did not check all the details. 

Novelty: The paper is novel. The technical contributions, e.g. how to construct the confidence set of the transition parameter in the adversarial setting; how to choose the imaginary next state; are all good ideas in my opinion.


Reproducibility: This is purely theoretical work with no empirical result. The proof looks reproducible to me as it is written clearly and not very hard to read.

Summary Of The Review:
My current recommendation is accept. 

The reasons are the following:

1. Solid contribution: this paper gives the answer to a quite challenging and important field of reinforcement learning theory. 

2. Some novelty: to solve certain technical challenges, some novel ideas have been proposed.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
### Strength

1. This paper studies a reasonable setting of "adversarial" RL with linear function approximation, and designs and proves the first algorithm with sqrt(K) regret. 
2. This paper goes beyond the classical VTR (value-targeted regression) framework of analysis for linear mixture MDP, coming up with a novel technique (imaginary roll out of state) to cope the challenge arouse in adversarial setting.

### Weakness

1. There are many settings of linear RL theory besides linear mixture MDP, such as linear MDP. Also, recently, there are unified framework such as Bellman-rank and bilinear classes for RL with general function approximation. In particular, these frameworks include linear mixture MDP as their special cases. While the theory of RL with function approximation is certainly an important topic, it could become much less important when the scope is restricted to linear mixture MDP.
2. This paper does not prove tight lower bound to complete the theoretical research in their setting.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper studies RL in linear mixture MDP with unknown transition, adversarial loss, and bandit feedback. This paper gives the first algorithm in this setting whose regret scales with sqrt(#episode) and supplements a lower bound, which matches the upper bound for the $d$ and $K$ dependency, where $K$ is #epsiode and $d$ is the ambient dimension of the linear mixture MDP.

Strength And Weaknesses:
### Strength

1. This paper studies a reasonable setting of "adversarial" RL with linear function approximation, and designs and proves the first algorithm with sqrt(K) regret. 
2. This paper goes beyond the classical VTR (value-targeted regression) framework of analysis for linear mixture MDP, coming up with a novel technique (imaginary roll out of state) to cope the challenge arouse in adversarial setting.

### Weakness

1. There are many settings of linear RL theory besides linear mixture MDP, such as linear MDP. Also, recently, there are unified framework such as Bellman-rank and bilinear classes for RL with general function approximation. In particular, these frameworks include linear mixture MDP as their special cases. While the theory of RL with function approximation is certainly an important topic, it could become much less important when the scope is restricted to linear mixture MDP.
2. This paper does not prove tight lower bound to complete the theoretical research in their setting.

Clarity Quality Novelty And Reproducibility:
The writing is clear and of good quality. No apparent typos are noticed.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper contains some novel results and techniques, but it could be made better if the authors could come up with more complete results.

reviews.text:
Summary of the Submission:
The authors tackle reinforcement learning in linear mixture Markov decision processes (MDPs) under adversarially chosen losses, unknown transitions, and bandit feedback. They propose what appears to be the first method in this context that achieves regret on the order of √K (where K is the number of episodes) and also derive a matching lower bound for the dependencies on the ambient dimension d and on K.

Major Contributions and Merits:
1. This work addresses a compelling variant of “adversarial” RL combined with linear function approximation, and it delivers the inaugural algorithm whose regret grows as √K.
2. The analysis goes beyond the standard value-targeted regression (VTR) toolkit used for linear mixture MDPs. In particular, the authors introduce a clever “imaginary rollout of states” to overcome obstacles specific to the adversarial loss setting.

Key Concerns and Limitations:
1. The focus on linear mixture MDPs sits within a broader landscape of linear RL theories—examples include the linear MDP model itself and unified constructs like Bellman-rank or bilinear frameworks that already encompass linear mixture MDPs as special cases. Restricting attention to just the linear mixture case may limit the overall impact of the results.
2. Although an informative lower bound is provided, it is not tight in every aspect. A fully matching lower bound would strengthen the completeness of the theoretical contribution in this adversarial-feedback scenario.

Clarity, Presentation, and Reproducibility:
The manuscript is clearly written and free of obvious typographical mistakes. The exposition is coherent, and the main ideas are accessible.

Overall Evaluation:
This paper makes interesting strides with novel analytical tools and a √K-regret guarantee in adversarial linear mixture MDPs. However, its narrow problem scope and the absence of a fully tight lower bound suggest that the theoretical treatment is not yet entirely comprehensive.

Final Recommendation:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studied the deployment efficient reinforcement learning in linear MDP. The authors proposed new algorithms with improved sample complexity bound than previous papers as well as the near-optimal deployment complexity.
reviews.strength_and_weaknesses:
# Strength

The sample complexity to state of the art, to my knowledge. The algorithm also seems interesting and somewhat novel.


# Weakness

I think the main issue is whether it is appropriate to claim the algorithm does not require the knowledge of $\lambda^*$. It seems to me that the main reason the algorithm does not use the knowledge of $\lambda^*$ is because the authors assumed that the accuracy level $\epsilon$ is smaller comparing with $\lambda^*$. From this perspective, it is kind of misleading to claim this paper get rid of knowledge of $\lambda^*$. Or maybe the authors should include discussion about how to avoid knowledge of $\lambda^*$ for arbitrary $\epsilon$.

Minor issues:

(1) the linear MDP is kind of restrictive, although more general than tabular setting.

(2) when $\lambda^*$ is extremely small, the algorithm can only success when choosing a extremely small $\epsilon$.

(3) algorithm seems not computationally efficient

# Weakness

It seems to me that the main reason the algorithm does not use the knowledge of $\lambda^*$ is because the authors assumed that the accuracy level $\epsilon$ is smaller comparing with $\lambda^*$. From this perspective, it is kind of misleading to claim this paper get rid of knowledge of $\lambda^*$. Or maybe the authors should include discussion about how to avoid knowledge of $\lambda^*$ for arbitrary $\epsilon$.
